topic: Part 1 scorecard — '0 Survive Scrutiny' sublabel (ISS-901 tightening, 81→24 words)
change_summary: ISS-901 applied: 0 Survive Scrutiny sublabel in Part 1 scorecard was compressed from 81 words to 24. New text: 'None produces a prediction that both derives from dome geometry and differs from standard physics. [What would change this?]'. Removed caveats: (a) 'This review does not assume globe geometry is correct' (b) 'Many referenced observations are real — the verdicts address interpretation, not the data'.
our_argument_summary: The 0 Survive Scrutiny scorecard card now carries a 24-word sublabel defining the survival criterion as 'derives from dome geometry AND differs from standard physics', linked to the falsifiability criteria in eg-falsifiability ('What would change this?'). Verdict of 0 survivors holds — but the tightened sublabel, read literally, is internally inconsistent with both the tag data in data/wins.json and with the expanded Condition 1 text in eg-falsifiability.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Not directly applicable — this is a section-level (scorecard sublabel) review. The '0' number itself is hardcoded in the scorecard; only {{TOTAL_WINS}}, {{DOME_CLAIMED_FAILURES}}, {{SILENT_FAILURES}}, {{DOME_CLAIMED_ACCURACY}}, and {{HONEST_ACCURACY}} are templated.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The '0' in 0 Survive Scrutiny is hardcoded. This is defensible (it is a structural argument, not a running count) but it means the sublabel's criterion MUST match what 0 counts. Currently it doesn't — see holes_found #1.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The 'What would change this?' link to eg-falsifiability provides the full four-condition falsifiability criteria, so the sublabel is formally defensible as a tag-line that delegates rigor to the detail section. But the delegation is undermined by the TLDR-vs-detail contradiction flagged below.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No code claims in the sublabel itself.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section-level review — tags apply to individual WINs.

holes_found.description: The sublabel 'None produces a prediction that both derives from dome geometry AND differs from standard physics' contradicts our own review data. Under the most natural reading (matching the code_analysis tags in data/wins.json), TWO claims satisfy both conditions: WIN-044 (Firmament Scaling Function from V12 geometry — derives_from_dome=true, relabels_standard=false) and WIN-057 (Two-zone disc topology — derives_from_dome=true, relabels_standard=false). Our OWN eg-falsifiability Condition 1 text acknowledges this explicitly: 'Currently only 2 of 69 claims even attempt a dome-geometry derivation (WIN-044, WIN-057), and both produce values that either match standard physics or contradict measurement.' So the detail section says 2 attempt the criterion; the scorecard sublabel (which the reader sees FIRST and may never expand) says 'None.' This is a direct TLDR-vs-detail contradiction. A careful dome defender would quote this: 'Your own review page lists WIN-044 and WIN-057 as dome-derived and differing from standard physics. Your scorecard says none do. Which is it?'
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the sublabel so the criterion matches the hardcoded '0'. Options (ordered by preference): (a) 'None produces a dome-derived prediction that both differs from standard physics and is confirmed by measurement.' (27 words with link); (b) 'No dome-derived prediction is both discriminating and confirmed.' (11 words with link) — most compact but may be cryptic on first read; (c) restore the third clause: 'derives from dome geometry, differs from standard physics, and survives internal consistency checks.' The key is to name the three-way bind (derives + differs + survives), not the two-way bind. The eg-falsifiability detail already names all three, so whichever wording is chosen must be consistent with it.

holes_found.description: The tightening removed the sentence 'This review does not assume globe geometry is correct — the strongest arguments (Parts 2 and 2b) use only the dome's own stated parameters and equations to show internal contradictions that require no external frame of reference.' This removal is defensible on pure scorecard-scannability grounds, but it also removes the reader's single clearest signal that the '0 Survive Scrutiny' verdict is not circular (is not just 'the globe is right, the dome is wrong'). In the dome community this is THE most common defense — 'you just assume the globe.' Without the self-test framing in the headline, a reader sees a bare assertion. The falsifiability link partially mitigates this, but the eg-falsifiability text leads with falsifiability conditions, not with a methodological disavowal of globe-assumption.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a single clause to the Part 1 scorecard-framing paragraph above the scorecard — not to the sublabel itself — stating 'This review uses the dome's own parameters and equations to evaluate its own claims,' or (b) add a visible assurance as a short framing line BELOW the scorecard (e.g., 'Findings use only the dome's own stated parameters; no globe assumption is made.'). Leaving the scorecard bare with only a falsifiability link is defensible but fragile against the most predictable dome-defender rebuttal.

holes_found.description: The removed caveat 'Many referenced observations are real — the verdicts address interpretation, not the data' was a genuine fairness concession. Its removal from the scorecard is fine (it is not the scorecard's job), but the concession is not reliably re-surfaced elsewhere in Part 1. The evidence-roadmap and eg-falsifiability do not explicitly say 'the underlying observational data cited by the dome is real.' A dome defender could claim we are dismissing observations in general.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence to the evidence-roadmap explicitly: 'Many of the underlying observations the dome cites (magnetic anomalies, neutrino flux, seismic timing, etc.) are real measurements we accept — our verdicts challenge the dome's interpretation and derivation, not the underlying data.' This is a one-line insertion that neutralizes a common strawman charge without bloating the scorecard.

stronger_arguments.description: Align the scorecard with the internal 3-way bind we already document everywhere else (Part 2 and 2b, Section 5 kill shots, eg-falsifiability Condition 1). The dome's predictions must be {dome-derived, discriminating, confirmed} to survive; currently 2 attempt the first two and 0 survive the third. Saying this cleanly is the strongest form of the argument and also reduces the attack surface.

Optional: cite the 2 that attempt derivation explicitly in the scorecard sublabel. Something like 'Only 2 of 69 claims attempt a dome-geometry derivation (WIN-044, WIN-057); both produce values that either match standard physics or contradict measurement.' This is longer (partially undoes the tightening) but it preempts the 'you ignored our dome-derived predictions' rebuttal by name. Trade-off: scannability vs. preemptive rigor. Recommend option (a) from hole #1 over this — citing WIN numbers in a scorecard sublabel is too detailed for the 3-second read.

kernel_of_truth.description: The tightening itself is a defensible UX move. 81 words is too long for a scorecard sublabel; 24 words respects the 3-second read. The core discriminating criterion (derives from dome AND differs from standard) is the right axis — our Part 2b is organized exactly around these two tags. Delegating rigor to 'What would change this?' is sound progressive disclosure. The removed caveats about globe-assumption and data-reality were not pulling their weight in the scorecard and genuinely belong in body text.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The tightening preserved the wrong two of the three logical axes. The correct 2-axis compression of the 3-axis criterion is 'dome-derived AND confirmed' (or 'dome-derived AND survives'), not 'dome-derived AND differs from standard.' The latter is true of 2 WINs but they fail survival, so the sublabel mislabels what '0' is counting.

text_fingerprint.sublabel_text: None produces a prediction that both derives from dome geometry and differs from standard physics. [What would change this?]

deeper_analysis: This is a textbook example of a section rewrite that gets UX right but logic almost-right. The rationale in the applied patch (suggested-patches-2026-04-13T22-13.json) says 'Retain the key discriminating criterion (derives from dome geometry + differs from standard physics).' Taken on its own terms, the patch succeeds at exactly what it claims. But the premise is wrong: the 'key discriminating criterion' is actually 3-valued, not 2-valued, and the old 81-word sublabel didn't state it correctly either. So the tightening inherits a pre-existing flaw from the old text — it isn't a regression, it's a failure to fix on the way through. Verifying against the data: I ran a tag audit over data/wins.json and found WIN-044 (derives_from_dome=true, relabels_standard=false, verdict='Self-Contradicted') and WIN-057 (derives_from_dome=true, relabels_standard=false, verdict='Misleading'). These are the exact two WINs eg-falsifiability names in its condition 1. So both data and prose agree there are 2; only the scorecard says 0 under a criterion that should return 2. The fix is a one-line edit. Because this is a headline element on the overview, the finding is major — not critical (no false citation, no fabricated data, no verdict-flip implication), but readers anchor on scorecard headlines and defenders hunt for TLDR-vs-detail mismatches. Fixing it also strengthens our position: we go from saying something slightly wrong in a scannable way to saying something exactly right in an equally scannable way. Kernel of truth for the dome defender: they CAN point to WIN-044 and WIN-057 as dome-derived predictions, and that's fine — we can and should acknowledge that publicly. The rebuttal is that derivation without survival doesn't survive.

citations_verified: WIN-044 tags match eg-falsifiability condition 1 naming (data/wins.json)
WIN-057 tags match eg-falsifiability condition 1 naming (data/wins.json)
Applied patch file matches queue reason (monitor/decisions/applied-patches/suggested-patches-2026-04-13T22-13.json)

tldr_review.tldr_role: Scorecard sublabel is effectively a 2-sentence TLDR for the 0 Survive Scrutiny card.
tldr_review.tldr_finding: The sublabel-as-TLDR fails the TLDR review: it simplifies the 3-axis criterion (derives + differs + survives) to a 2-axis criterion (derives + differs), under which the answer is 2, not 0. Scored as major per Severity Guidelines (TLDR factual errors that mislead are major).
